# Compound Agent Active

> **Context Recovery**: Run `npx ca prime` after compaction, clear, or new session

## CLI Commands (ALWAYS USE THESE)

**You MUST use CLI commands for lesson management:**

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `npx ca search "query"` | Search lessons - MUST call before architectural decisions; use anytime you need context |
| `npx ca learn "insight"` | Capture lessons - call AFTER corrections or discoveries |

## Core Constraints

**Default**: Use CLI commands for lesson management
**Prohibited**: NEVER edit .claude/lessons/ files directly

**Default**: Propose lessons freely after corrections
**Prohibited**: NEVER propose without quality gate (novel + specific; prefer actionable)

## Retrieval Protocol

You MUST call `npx ca search` BEFORE:
- Architectural decisions or complex planning
- Implementing patterns you've done before in this repo

**NEVER skip search for complex decisions.** Past mistakes will repeat.

Beyond mandatory triggers, use these commands freely — they are lightweight queries, not heavyweight operations. Uncertain about a pattern? `ca search`. The cost of an unnecessary search is near-zero; the cost of a missed one can be hours.

## Capture Protocol

Run `npx ca learn` AFTER:
- User corrects you ("no", "wrong", "actually...")
- You self-correct after iteration failures
- Test fails then you fix it

**Quality gate** (must pass before capturing):
- Novel (not already stored)
- Specific (clear guidance)
- Actionable (preferred, not mandatory)

**Workflow**: Search BEFORE deciding, capture AFTER learning.

# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when .beads/ detected

# 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. git commit -m "..."     (commit code)
[ ] 4. git push                (push to remote)
```

**NEVER skip this.** Work is not done until pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: beads auto-commit to Dolt, run `git push` at session end
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
  - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
- `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
- `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
- `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
- `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
- **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
- **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

### Dependencies & Blocking
- `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
- `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
- `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

### Sync & Collaboration
- `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
- `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads from Dolt remote
- `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

### Project Health
- `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
- `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)
- `bd doctor --check=conventions` - Check for convention drift (lint, stale, orphans)

### Quality Tools
- `bd create --validate` - Check description has required sections
- `bd create --acceptance="criteria"` - Set acceptance criteria (checked by --validate)
- `bd create --design="decisions"` - Record design decisions
- `bd create --notes="context"` - Add supplementary notes
- `bd config set validation.on-create warn` - Auto-validate on every create
- `bd lint` - Check existing issues for missing sections

### Lifecycle & Hygiene
- `bd defer <id> --until="date"` - Defer work to a future date
- `bd supersede <id> --with=<new-id>` - Mark issue as superseded
- `bd close <id> --suggest-next` - Show newly unblocked issues after closing
- `bd stale` - Find issues with no recent activity
- `bd orphans` - Find issues with broken dependencies
- `bd preflight` - Pre-PR checks (lint, stale, orphans)
- `bd human <id>` - Flag for human decision (list/respond/dismiss)

### Structured Workflows
- `bd formula list` - See available workflow templates
- `bd mol pour <name>` - Start structured workflow from formula

## Common Workflows

**Starting work:**
```bash
bd ready           # Find available work
bd show <id>       # Review issue details
bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
```

**Completing work:**
```bash
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

○ dark-research-lab-q1f [EPIC] · Epic 1: Go Binary Bootstrap   [● P1 · OPEN]
Owner: Nathan Delacrétaz · Type: epic
Created: 2026-04-02 · Updated: 2026-04-03

DESCRIPTION
Fork compound-agent Go binary (ca -> drl), package for PyPI distribution.

## Scope
IN: Fork go/ directory from ca, rename all 'ca' references to 'drl', update Go module path, update embedded template manifest with tier annotations (infrastructure/core/style), tiered drl setup command (see below), Python packaging wrapper (pyproject.toml, platform-specific wheels, entry point), cross-compile build script, smoke test.
OUT: Template content (skills, agents, commands written in other epics), new Go functionality (drl index), PyPI publishing automation.

## Setup Tiers
The template manifest must annotate each file with a tier:
- infrastructure: hooks, settings.json, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, paper/, src/, docs/, literature/, tests/, commands
- core: structural workflow skills (architect, spec, plan, work, review, synthesis, flavor, onboard) + core agents (analyst, reproducibility-verifier)
- style: field-dependent skills (lit-review) + style agents (methodology-reviewer, coherence-reviewer, writing-quality-reviewer, citation-checker, literature-analyst, robustness-checker)

Behavior:
- drl setup (empty repo): install ALL tiers
- drl setup (existing repo): install infrastructure only, skip skills/agents
- drl setup --core-skill: infrastructure + core skills/agents
- drl setup --all-skill: everything, overwriting flavor customizations

## Distribution Model
Ship Go binary inside Python wheels (same pattern as ruff/uv):
- pyproject.toml with [project.scripts] drl = 'drl:main'
- Python wrapper finds and executes embedded Go binary
- Platform-specific wheels per OS/arch
- Install: uv pip install drl

## EARS Subset
- U1: CLI distributed as PyPI package with Go binary named drl
- U4: Preserve all compound-agent subsystems
- E1/E1a/E1b: Tiered setup behavior

## Verification Contract
- drl --version prints version
- drl setup in empty repo creates ALL files
- drl setup in existing repo only updates infrastructure
- drl setup --core-skill updates core skills but not style
- drl setup --all-skill overwrites everything
- uv pip install drl works on macOS ARM64

BLOCKS
  ← ○ dark-research-lab-1if: (EPIC) Epic 4: Literature RAG Pipeline ● P2
  ← ○ dark-research-lab-3ir: (EPIC) Epic 3: Paper + Code Scaffolding ● P1
  ← ○ dark-research-lab-7s3: (EPIC) Epic 2: Workflow Infrastructure ● P1
  ← ○ dark-research-lab-rjx: (EPIC) Epic 6: Research Architect + Agent Definitions ● P1
  ← ○ dark-research-lab-v16: (EPIC) Epic IV: Integration Verification ● P2

file.content:
---
name: Compound Agent Reference
description: Key patterns from Nathandela/compound-agent to reuse - file structure, skills, hooks, architect
type: reference
---

Source repo: github.com/Nathandela/compound-agent

Key patterns to copy and adapt:
- **Skill-as-instruction-file**: Commands are thin wrappers that Read SKILL.md. Survives context compaction.
- **5-phase cook-it**: Spec-Dev -> Plan -> Work -> Review -> Compound. Phase gates enforce sequencing.
- **Phase guard hook**: PreToolUse blocks Edit/Write if current phase's SKILL.md hasn't been read.
- **Architect skill**: 6-phase decomposition (Socratic, Spec, Decompose, Materialize, Launch, Polish) with 5 human gates and parallel subagent convoy.
- **Advisory fleet**: Spawns external model CLIs (claude, gemini, codex) as background processes for multi-perspective evaluation.
- **Infinity loop**: `ca loop` generates bash script processing beads epics in dependency order via screen sessions.
- **Memory**: JSONL + SQLite FTS5 + Rust ONNX embeddings. Auto-injected at SessionStart and PreCompact.
- **Hooks**: 7 hooks (SessionStart, PreCompact, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUseFailure, PostToolUse, PreToolUse, Stop).
- **Templates embedded**: Go's embed package bundles all templates. `ca setup` installs them.

Go binary structure: `go/cmd/ca/main.go` + `go/internal/` (cli, capture, compound, embed, hook, knowledge, memory, retrieval, search, setup, storage, telemetry).

compound-agent — learning system for Claude Code

Usage:
  ca [command]

Available Commands:
  about           Show version and project info
  audit           Run audit checks against the codebase
  capture         Programmatic lesson capture
  check-plan      Check a plan against learned lessons
  compact         Remove tombstones and rewrite JSONL
  completion      Generate the autocompletion script for the specified shell
  compound        Synthesize cross-cutting patterns from lessons
  delete          Soft delete lessons (creates tombstone)
  detect          Auto-detect corrections from input
  doctor          Check compound-agent health
  download-model  Download the embedding model
  export          Export lessons as JSONL to stdout
  feedback        Open GitHub Discussions to share feedback or report issues
  health          Show telemetry health stats (avg hook latency, retrieval counts)
  help            Help about any command
  hooks           Hook management commands
  import          Import lessons from a JSONL file
  improve         Generate improvement loop script
  index-docs      Index documentation files for knowledge search
  info            Show a structured overview of hooks, skills, phases, telemetry, and lessons
  init            Initialize compound-agent in this repository
  install-beads   Install the beads CLI via the official install script
  knowledge       Search indexed documentation
  learn           Manually capture a lesson

1	package main
2	
3	import (
4		"log/slog"
5		"os"
6	
7		"github.com/nathandelacretaz/compound-agent/internal/cli"
8		"github.com/nathandelacretaz/compound-agent/internal/hook"
9		"github.com/nathandelacretaz/compound-agent/internal/storage"
10		"github.com/nathandelacretaz/compound-agent/internal/util"
11		"github.com/spf13/cobra"
12	)
13	
14	// buildHooksCmd creates the "hooks run" command tree.
15	func buildHooksCmd() *cobra.Command {
16		hooksCmd := &cobra.Command{
17			Use:   "hooks",
18			Short: "Hook management commands",
19		}
20		runCmd := &cobra.Command{
21			Use:   "run [hook-name]",
22			Short: "Run a hook handler",
23			Args:  cobra.MaximumNArgs(1),
24			Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
25				hookName := ""
26				if len(args) > 0 {
27					hookName = args[0]
28				}
29	
30				// Open DB for telemetry; fall back to RunHook without telemetry if DB unavailable.
31				repoRoot := util.GetRepoRoot()
32				db, err := storage.OpenRepoDB(repoRoot)
33				if err != nil {
34					slog.Debug("telemetry db unavailable", "error", err)
35					os.Exit(hook.RunHook(hookName, os.Stdin, os.Stdout))
36				}
37	
38				exitCode := hook.RunHookWithTelemetry(hookName, os.Stdin, os.Stdout, db)
39				db.Close()
40				os.Exit(exitCode)
41			},
42		}
43		hooksCmd.AddCommand(runCmd)
44		return hooksCmd
45	}
46	
47	func main() {
48		var (
49			verbose bool
50			quiet   bool
51		)
52	
53		rootCmd := &cobra.Command{
54			Use:   "ca",
55			Short: "compound-agent — learning system for Claude Code",
56			PersistentPreRun: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
57				if verbose {
58					slog.SetDefault(slog.New(slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stderr, &slog.HandlerOptions{
59						Level: slog.LevelDebug,
60					})))
61				} else {
62					slog.SetDefault(slog.New(slog.NewTextHandler(os.Stderr, &slog.HandlerOptions{
63						Level: slog.LevelWarn,
64					})))
65				}
66			},
67		}
68		rootCmd.SetOut(os.Stdout)
69		rootCmd.SetErr(os.Stderr)
70	
71		rootCmd.PersistentFlags().BoolVarP(&verbose, "verbose", "v", false, "enable verbose (debug-level) logging")
72		rootCmd.PersistentFlags().BoolVarP(&quiet, "quiet", "q", false, "suppress non-essential output")
73	
74		rootCmd.AddCommand(buildHooksCmd())
75		cli.RegisterCommands(rootCmd)
76	
77		if err := rootCmd.Execute(); err != nil {
78			os.Exit(1)
79		}
80	}

1	DIST      := dist
2	VERSION   := $(shell git describe --tags --always --dirty 2>/dev/null || echo "dev")
3	COMMIT    := $(shell git rev-parse --short HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
4	LDFLAGS   := -s -w -X github.com/nathandelacretaz/compound-agent/internal/build.Version=$(VERSION) \
5	             -X github.com/nathandelacretaz/compound-agent/internal/build.Commit=$(COMMIT)
6	RUST_DIR  := ../rust/embed-daemon
7	
8	PLATFORMS := darwin-arm64 darwin-amd64 linux-arm64 linux-amd64 windows-amd64 windows-arm64
9	
10	.PHONY: build build-release build-all build-daemon build-daemon-all test test-integration lint clean
11	
12	# Build for local platform (development).
13	build:
14		go build -o ca ./cmd/ca
15	
16	# Build for local platform with version injection and stripped symbols.
17	build-release:
18		go build -ldflags '$(LDFLAGS)' -o $(DIST)/ca ./cmd/ca
19	
20	# Cross-compile Go binary for all platforms.
21	# Pure Go (CGO_ENABLED=0) — no C cross-compiler needed.
22	build-all: $(DIST)
23		@for platform in $(PLATFORMS); do \
24			os=$${platform%%-*}; \
25			arch=$${platform##*-}; \
26			ext=""; \
27			if [ "$$os" = "windows" ]; then ext=".exe"; fi; \
28			echo "Building ca-$${platform}$${ext}..."; \
29			CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=$$os GOARCH=$$arch \
30				go build -ldflags '$(LDFLAGS)' \
31				-o $(DIST)/ca-$$platform$$ext ./cmd/ca || exit 1; \
32		done
33	
34	# Build Rust embed-daemon for local platform.
35	build-daemon:
36		cd $(RUST_DIR) && cargo build --release
37		@mkdir -p $(DIST)
38		cp $(RUST_DIR)/target/release/ca-embed $(DIST)/ca-embed
39	
40	# Cross-compile Rust embed-daemon for all platforms.
41	# Requires rustup target add for each target triple.
42	build-daemon-all: $(DIST)
43		@for platform in $(PLATFORMS); do \
44			case $$platform in \
45				windows-*) continue ;; \
46				darwin-arm64) triple="aarch64-apple-darwin" ;; \
47				darwin-amd64) triple="x86_64-apple-darwin" ;; \
48				linux-arm64)  triple="aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu" ;; \
49				linux-amd64)  triple="x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu" ;; \
50			esac; \
51			echo "Building embed-daemon-$$platform ($$triple)..."; \
52			cd $(RUST_DIR) && cargo build --release --target $$triple || exit 1; \
53			cp $(RUST_DIR)/target/$$triple/release/ca-embed $(DIST)/ca-embed-$$platform; \
54		done
55

64

66		rm -rf $(DIST) ca
67		rm -rf $(RUST_DIR)/target
68	
69	$(DIST):
70		@mkdir -p $(DIST)

12	// InitOptions controls what init creates.
13	type InitOptions struct {
14		SkipHooks     bool
15		SkipTemplates bool   // Skip installing agent/command/skill/doc templates.
16		BinaryPath    string // Path to the Go binary for hook commands. Empty = npx fallback.
17	}
18	
19	// InitResult reports what init did.
20	type InitResult struct {
21		Success             bool
22		HooksInstalled      bool
23		HooksUpgraded       bool
24		DirsCreated         []string
25		FilesCreated        []string
26		AgentsInstalled     int
27		AgentsUpdated       int
28		CommandsInstalled   int
29		CommandsUpdated     int
30		SkillsInstalled     int
31		SkillsUpdated       int
32		RoleSkillsInstalled int
33		RoleSkillsUpdated   int
34		DocsInstalled       int
35		DocsUpdated         int
36		ResearchInstalled   int
37		ResearchUpdated     int
38		TemplatesPruned     int
39		AgentsMdUpdated     bool
40		ClaudeMdUpdated     bool
41		PluginCreated       bool
42		PluginUpdated       bool
43	}
44	
45	// initDirectories creates the .claude/ directory structure and index.jsonl.
46	func initDirectories(repoRoot string, result *InitResult) error {
47		dirs := []string{
48			filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude"),
49			filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude", "lessons"),
50			filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude", ".cache"),
51			filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude", "agents", "compound"),
52			filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude", "commands", "compound"),
53			filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude", "skills", "compound"),
54		}
55	
56		for _, dir := range dirs {
57			_, statErr := os.Stat(dir)
58			if err := os.MkdirAll(dir, 0755); err != nil {
59				return fmt.Errorf("create directory %s: %w", dir, err)
60			}
61			if os.IsNotExist(statErr) {
62				result.DirsCreated = append(result.DirsCreated, dir)
63			}
64		}
65	
66		indexPath := filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude", "lessons", "index.jsonl")
67		if _, err := os.Stat(indexPath); os.IsNotExist(err) {
68			if err := os.WriteFile(indexPath, []byte{}, 0644); err != nil {
69				return fmt.Errorf("create index.jsonl: %w", err)
70			}
71			result.FilesCreated = append(result.FilesCreated, indexPath)
72		}
73	
74		return EnsureGitignore(repoRoot)
75	}
76	
77	// initHooks installs or upgrades Claude Code hooks in settings.json.
78	func initHooks(repoRoot string, binaryPath string, result *InitResult) error {
79		settingsPath := filepath.Join(repoRoot, ".claude", "settings.json")
80		settings, err := ReadClaudeSettings(settingsPath)
81		if err != nil {
82			return fmt.Errorf("read settings: %w", err)
83		}
84	
85		needsInstall := !HasAllHooks(settings)
86		needsUpgrade := HooksNeedUpgrade(settings, binaryPath)
87		needsDedupe := HooksNeedDedupe(settings)
88	
89		if needsInstall || needsUpgrade || needsDedupe {
90			AddAllHooks(settings, binaryPath)
91			if err := WriteClaudeSettings(settingsPath, settings); err != nil {
92				return fmt.Errorf("write settings: %w", err)
93			}
94			result.HooksUpgraded = needsUpgrade && !needsInstall
95		}
96		result.HooksInstalled = true
97		return nil
98	}

98	// ReadClaudeSettings reads and parses a Claude Code settings.json file.
99	// Returns empty map if file does not exist.
100	func ReadClaudeSettings(path string) (map[string]any, error) {
101		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
102		if os.IsNotExist(err) {
103			return map[string]any{}, nil
104		}
105		if err != nil {
106			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read settings: %w", err)
107		}
108		var settings map[string]any
109		if err := json.Unmarshal(data, &settings); err != nil {
110			return nil, fmt.Errorf("parse settings: %w", err)
111		}
112		return settings, nil
113	}
114	
115	// WriteClaudeSettings writes settings.json atomically (write to temp, then rename).
116	func WriteClaudeSettings(path string, settings map[string]any) error {
117		dir := filepath.Dir(path)
118		if err := os.MkdirAll(dir, 0755); err != nil {
119			return fmt.Errorf("create directory: %w", err)
120		}
121		data, err := json.MarshalIndent(settings, "", "  ")
122		if err != nil {
123			return fmt.Errorf("marshal settings: %w", err)
124		}
125		data = append(data, '\n')
126	
127		tmpPath := path + ".tmp"
128		if err := os.WriteFile(tmpPath, data, 0644); err != nil {
129			return fmt.Errorf("write temp file: %w", err)
130		}
131		return os.Rename(tmpPath, path)
132	}

137		if binaryPath != "" {
138			return fmt.Sprintf("%s hooks run %s 2>/dev/null || true", util.ShellEscape(binaryPath), hookName)
139		}
140		return fmt.Sprintf("npx ca hooks run %s 2>/dev/null || true", hookName)
141	}

### 4. SETUP COMMAND & TEMPLATE INSTALLATION

**Entry Point**: `/tmp/ca-source/go/internal/cli/commands_setup.go`

**Key Commands**:

- `setup` - Setup compound-agent (wrapper around init with different messaging)
- `setup claude` - Install Claude Code hooks
- `doctor` - Check compound-agent health

**How Templates Are Installed**:

1. Templates are embedded at compile time in `/tmp/ca-source/go/internal/setup/templates/embed.go`
2. **Embed directives** (line 12-37):
   ```go
   //go:embed agents/*.md
   var agentsFS embed.FS
   
   //go:embed commands/*.md
   var commandsFS embed.FS
   
   //go:embed skills
   var skillsFS embed.FS
   ```

   - `AgentRoleSkills()` - Returns role-based SKILL.md files

   - `DocTemplates()` - Returns doc .md files
   - `ResearchDocs()` - Returns research documentation

4. **Installation Flow** (init.go):
   - Creates `.claude/` directory structure
   - Creates empty `lessons/index.jsonl`
   - Installs Claude Code hooks (if not skipped)
   - Calls `installTemplates()` which:

     - Ensures CLAUDE.md reference
     - Creates plugin.json manifest
     - Installs all template groups (agents, commands, skills, docs, research)

Scope: Fork go/ directory, rename all 'ca' references to 'drl', update Go module path, embedded template manifest with tier annotations, tiered drl setup command, Python packaging wrapper, cross-compile build script, smoke test.

# Plan

**MANDATORY FIRST STEP -- NON-NEGOTIABLE**: Use the Read tool to open and read `.claude/skills/compound/plan/SKILL.md` NOW. Do NOT proceed until you have read the complete skill file. It contains the full workflow you must follow.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: Plan
3	description: Decompose work into small testable tasks with clear dependencies
4	phase: plan
5	---
6	
7	# Plan Skill
8	
9	## Overview
10	Create a concrete implementation plan by decomposing work into small, testable tasks with dependencies and acceptance criteria.
11	
12	## Methodology
13	1. Read the spec from the epic description (`bd show <epic>`) for EARS requirements, decisions, and open questions. Verify its type is `epic` -- if it was created as `task`, fix with `bd update <id> --type=epic`
14	2. Search memory with `ca search` and docs with `ca knowledge "relevant topic"` for architectural patterns and past mistakes
15	3. Spawn **subagents** via Task tool in parallel for research (lightweight, no inter-agent coordination):
16	   - Available agents: `.claude/agents/compound/repo-analyst.md`, `memory-analyst.md`
17	   - For complex features, deploy MULTIPLE analysts per domain area
18	   - Synthesize all findings before decomposing into tasks
19	4. For decisions requiring deep technical grounding, invoke the **researcher skill** to produce a survey document. Review findings before decomposing into tasks.
20	5. Synthesize research findings into a coherent approach. Flag conflicts between ADRs and proposed plan.
21	6. Use `AskUserQuestion` to resolve ambiguities, conflicting constraints, or priority trade-offs before decomposing
22	7. Decompose into tasks small enough to verify individually
23	8. Define acceptance criteria for each task
24	9. Ensure each task traces back to a spec requirement for traceability
25	10. **Generate Acceptance Criteria table**: Extract testable criteria from EARS requirements and append to the epic description. Use this format:
26	
27	    ```markdown
28	    ## Acceptance Criteria
29	    | ID | Source Req | Criterion | Verification Method |
30	    |----|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
31	    | AC-1 | EARS-N | When X, system shall Y within Z | unit test / manual / integration |
32	    ```
33	
34	    Rules:
35	    - Each EARS requirement MUST map to at least one AC row
36	    - Criteria MUST be testable (no vague adjectives like "fast" or "good")
37	    - Verification method MUST be specified
38	    - Write the AC table to the epic via `bd update <epic-id> --description="<existing desc + AC section>"`
39	    - The AC section is **append-only** after plan phase; review annotates pass/fail
40	11. **Generate Verification Contract**: Derive a minimal, per-epic definition of done and append it to the epic description after the Acceptance Criteria section. Use this format:
41	
42	    ```markdown
43	    ## Verification Contract
44	    Profile: webapp | api | cli | library | service | mixed
45	    Surfaces:
46	    - ui_surface
47	    Risks:
48	    - user_visible_quality
49	    Required evidence:
50	    - test
51	    - lint
52	    - build
53	    ```
54	
55	    Rules:
56	    - Detect the profile from repo signals plus the epic's actual scope. If architect/spec work noted a default profile, use it as advisory input only.
57	    - Keep the contract **local to this epic**. Do NOT create repo-global config for this.
58	    - Start from a baseline and then tailor:
59	      - `webapp` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `browser_evidence`
60	      - `api` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `contract_checks`
61	      - `cli` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `help_version_check`, `command_transcript`
62	      - `library` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `examples_run`, `public_api_review`
63	      - `service` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `config_validation`
64	    - Add contract-specific evidence from touched surfaces and risks:
65	      - `ui_surface` -> `responsive_check`, `edge_states_check`, `console_network_clean`, `a11y_smoke`, `design_craft_check`
66	      - `public_api` -> `contract_examples`, `backward_compat_review`
67	      - `persistence_schema` -> `roundtrip_test`, `migration_check`, `backcompat_check`
68	      - `packaging_or_distribution` -> `package_build`, `install_smoke`
69	      - `docs_or_examples` -> `docs_examples_sync`
70	      - `auth_or_security` -> `auth_failure_paths`
71	      - `performance_sensitive` -> `performance_budget_check`
72	    - Use a small, explicit vocabulary for `Surfaces` and `Risks`; prefer consistency over novelty.
73	    - If the profile is ambiguous **and** the choice materially changes required evidence, resolve it with `AskUserQuestion` once before finalizing the plan.
74	    - Write the Verification Contract to the epic via `bd update <epic-id> --description="<existing desc + AC section + Verification Contract section>"`
75	    - The Verification Contract is **append-only** after plan; review may escalate it explicitly if risk was underestimated.
76	12. Map dependencies between tasks
77	13. Create beads issues: `bd create --title="..." --type=task`
78	14. Create review and compound blocking tasks (`bd create` + `bd dep add`) that depend on work tasks — these survive compaction and surface via `bd ready` after work completes
79	
80	## Memory Integration
81	- Run `ca search` and `ca knowledge "relevant topic"` for patterns related to the feature area
82	- Look for past planning mistakes (missing dependencies, unclear criteria)
83	- Check for preferred architectural patterns in this codebase
84	
85	## Docs Integration
86	- Spawn docs-analyst to scan `docs/` for relevant specs, standards, and research
87	- Check `docs/decisions/` for existing ADRs that constrain or inform the plan
88	- If the plan contradicts an ADR, flag it for the user before proceeding
89	
90	## Common Pitfalls
91	- Creating too many fine-grained tasks (aim for 3-7 per feature)
92	- Unclear acceptance criteria ("make it work" is not a criterion)
93	- Missing dependencies between tasks
94	- Not checking memory for past architectural decisions
95	- Not reviewing existing ADRs and docs for constraints
96	- Making architectural decisions without research backing (use the researcher skill for complex domains)
97	- Planning implementation details too early (stay at task level)
98	- Not generating Acceptance Criteria table from EARS requirements
99	- Not generating a Verification Contract, leaving later phases to guess what evidence is required
100	
101	## Quality Criteria
102	- Each task has clear acceptance criteria
103	- Dependencies are mapped and no circular dependencies exist
104	- Tasks are ordered so each can be verified independently
105	- Memory was searched for relevant patterns and past mistakes
106	- Existing docs and ADRs were checked for constraints
107	- Ambiguities resolved via `AskUserQuestion` before decomposing
108	- Complexity estimates are realistic (no "should be quick")
109	- Each task traces back to a spec requirement
110	- **Acceptance Criteria table generated and appended to epic description**
111	- **Verification Contract generated and appended to epic description**
112	
113	## POST-PLAN VERIFICATION -- MANDATORY
114	After creating all tasks, verify review and compound tasks exist:
115	- Run `bd list --status=open` and check for a "Review:" task and a "Compound:" task
116	- If either is missing, CREATE THEM NOW. The plan is NOT complete without these gates.
117	- **Verify AC table**: Run `bd show <epic-id>` and confirm the `## Acceptance Criteria` section exists in the description. If missing, the plan is NOT complete.
118	- **Verify contract**: Run `bd show <epic-id>` and confirm the `## Verification Contract` section exists in the description. If missing, the plan is NOT complete.
119

file.content:
---
name: Plan
description: Decompose work into small testable tasks with clear dependencies
phase: plan
---

# Plan Skill

## Overview
Create a concrete implementation plan by decomposing work into small, testable tasks with dependencies and acceptance criteria.

## Methodology
1. Read the spec from the epic description (`bd show <epic>`) for EARS requirements, decisions, and open questions. Verify its type is `epic` -- if it was created as `task`, fix with `bd update <id> --type=epic`
2. Search memory with `ca search` and docs with `ca knowledge "relevant topic"` for architectural patterns and past mistakes
3. Spawn **subagents** via Task tool in parallel for research (lightweight, no inter-agent coordination):
   - Available agents: `.claude/agents/compound/repo-analyst.md`, `memory-analyst.md`
   - For complex features, deploy MULTIPLE analysts per domain area
   - Synthesize all findings before decomposing into tasks
4. For decisions requiring deep technical grounding, invoke the **researcher skill** to produce a survey document. Review findings before decomposing into tasks.
5. Synthesize research findings into a coherent approach. Flag conflicts between ADRs and proposed plan.
6. Use `AskUserQuestion` to resolve ambiguities, conflicting constraints, or priority trade-offs before decomposing
7. Decompose into tasks small enough to verify individually
8. Define acceptance criteria for each task
9. Ensure each task traces back to a spec requirement for traceability
10. **Generate Acceptance Criteria table**: Extract testable criteria from EARS requirements and append to the epic description. Use this format:

    ```markdown
    ## Acceptance Criteria
    | ID | Source Req | Criterion | Verification Method |
    |----|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
    | AC-1 | EARS-N | When X, system shall Y within Z | unit test / manual / integration |
    ```

    Rules:
    - Each EARS requirement MUST map to at least one AC row
    - Criteria MUST be testable (no vague adjectives like "fast" or "good")
    - Verification method MUST be specified
    - Write the AC table to the epic via `bd update <epic-id> --description="<existing desc + AC section>"`
    - The AC section is **append-only** after plan phase; review annotates pass/fail
11. **Generate Verification Contract**: Derive a minimal, per-epic definition of done and append it to the epic description after the Acceptance Criteria section. Use this format:

    ```markdown
    ## Verification Contract
    Profile: webapp | api | cli | library | service | mixed
    Surfaces:
    - ui_surface
    Risks:
    - user_visible_quality
    Required evidence:
    - test
    - lint
    - build
    ```

    Rules:
    - Detect the profile from repo signals plus the epic's actual scope. If architect/spec work noted a default profile, use it as advisory input only.
    - Keep the contract **local to this epic**. Do NOT create repo-global config for this.
    - Start from a baseline and then tailor:
      - `webapp` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `browser_evidence`
      - `api` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `contract_checks`
      - `cli` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `help_version_check`, `command_transcript`
      - `library` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `examples_run`, `public_api_review`
      - `service` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `config_validation`
    - Add contract-specific evidence from touched surfaces and risks:
      - `ui_surface` -> `responsive_check`, `edge_states_check`, `console_network_clean`, `a11y_smoke`, `design_craft_check`
      - `public_api` -> `contract_examples`, `backward_compat_review`
      - `persistence_schema` -> `roundtrip_test`, `migration_check`, `backcompat_check`
      - `packaging_or_distribution` -> `package_build`, `install_smoke`
      - `docs_or_examples` -> `docs_examples_sync`
      - `auth_or_security` -> `auth_failure_paths`
      - `performance_sensitive` -> `performance_budget_check`
    - Use a small, explicit vocabulary for `Surfaces` and `Risks`; prefer consistency over novelty.
    - If the profile is ambiguous **and** the choice materially changes required evidence, resolve it with `AskUserQuestion` once before finalizing the plan.
    - Write the Verification Contract to the epic via `bd update <epic-id> --description="<existing desc + AC section + Verification Contract section>"`
    - The Verification Contract is **append-only** after plan; review may escalate it explicitly if risk was underestimated.
12. Map dependencies between tasks
13. Create beads issues: `bd create --title="..." --type=task`
14. Create review and compound blocking tasks (`bd create` + `bd dep add`) that depend on work tasks — these survive compaction and surface via `bd ready` after work completes

## Memory Integration
- Run `ca search` and `ca knowledge "relevant topic"` for patterns related to the feature area
- Look for past planning mistakes (missing dependencies, unclear criteria)
- Check for preferred architectural patterns in this codebase

## Docs Integration
- Spawn docs-analyst to scan `docs/` for relevant specs, standards, and research
- Check `docs/decisions/` for existing ADRs that constrain or inform the plan
- If the plan contradicts an ADR, flag it for the user before proceeding

## Common Pitfalls
- Creating too many fine-grained tasks (aim for 3-7 per feature)
- Unclear acceptance criteria ("make it work" is not a criterion)
- Missing dependencies between tasks
- Not checking memory for past architectural decisions
- Not reviewing existing ADRs and docs for constraints
- Making architectural decisions without research backing (use the researcher skill for complex domains)
- Planning implementation details too early (stay at task level)
- Not generating Acceptance Criteria table from EARS requirements
- Not generating a Verification Contract, leaving later phases to guess what evidence is required

## Quality Criteria
- Each task has clear acceptance criteria
- Dependencies are mapped and no circular dependencies exist
- Tasks are ordered so each can be verified independently
- Memory was searched for relevant patterns and past mistakes
- Existing docs and ADRs were checked for constraints
- Ambiguities resolved via `AskUserQuestion` before decomposing
- Complexity estimates are realistic (no "should be quick")
- Each task traces back to a spec requirement
- **Acceptance Criteria table generated and appended to epic description**
- **Verification Contract generated and appended to epic description**

## POST-PLAN VERIFICATION -- MANDATORY
After creating all tasks, verify review and compound tasks exist:
- Run `bd list --status=open` and check for a "Review:" task and a "Compound:" task
- If either is missing, CREATE THEM NOW. The plan is NOT complete without these gates.
- **Verify AC table**: Run `bd show <epic-id>` and confirm the `## Acceptance Criteria` section exists in the description. If missing, the plan is NOT complete.
- **Verify contract**: Run `bd show <epic-id>` and confirm the `## Verification Contract` section exists in the description. If missing, the plan is NOT complete.

1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
4	
5	## Quick Reference
6	
7	```bash
8	bd ready              # Find available work
9	bd show <id>          # View issue details
10	bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work atomically
11	bd close <id>         # Complete work
12	bd dolt push          # Push beads data to remote
13	```
14	
15	## Non-Interactive Shell Commands
16	
17	**ALWAYS use non-interactive flags** with file operations to avoid hanging on confirmation prompts.
18	
19	Shell commands like `cp`, `mv`, and `rm` may be aliased to include `-i` (interactive) mode on some systems, causing the agent to hang indefinitely waiting for y/n input.
20	
21	**Use these forms instead:**
22	```bash
23	# Force overwrite without prompting
24	cp -f source dest           # NOT: cp source dest
25	mv -f source dest           # NOT: mv source dest
26	rm -f file                  # NOT: rm file
27	
28	# For recursive operations
29	rm -rf directory            # NOT: rm -r directory
30	cp -rf source dest          # NOT: cp -r source dest
31	```
32	
33	**Other commands that may prompt:**
34	- `scp` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` for non-interactive
35	- `ssh` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` to fail instead of prompting
36	- `apt-get` - use `-y` flag
37	- `brew` - use `HOMEBREW_NO_AUTO_UPDATE=1` env var
38	
39	<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION profile:full hash:d4f96305 -->
40	## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
41	
42	**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
43	
44	### Why bd?
45	
46	- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
47	- Git-friendly: Dolt-powered version control with native sync
48	- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
49	- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
50	
51	### Quick Start
52	
53	**Check for ready work:**
54	
55	```bash
56	bd ready --json
57	```
58	
59	**Create new issues:**
60	
61	```bash
62	bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
63	bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
64	```
65	
66	**Claim and update:**
67	
68	```bash
69	bd update <id> --claim --json
70	bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
71	```
72	
73	**Complete work:**
74	
75	```bash
76	bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
77	```
78	
79	### Issue Types
80	
81	- `bug` - Something broken
82	- `feature` - New functionality
83	- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
84	- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
85	- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
86	
87	### Priorities
88	
89	- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
90	- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
91	- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
92	- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
93	- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
94	
95	### Workflow for AI Agents
96	
97	1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
98	2. **Claim your task atomically**: `bd update <id> --claim`
99	3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
100	4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
101	   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
102	5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
103	
104	### Auto-Sync
105	
106	bd automatically syncs via Dolt:
107	
108	- Each write auto-commits to Dolt history
109	- Use `bd dolt push`/`bd dolt pull` for remote sync
110	- No manual export/import needed!
111	
112	### Important Rules
113	
114	- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
115	- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
116	- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
117	- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
118	- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
119	- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
120	- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
121	
122	For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
123	
124	## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
125	
126	**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
127	
128	**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
129	
130	1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
131	2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
132	3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
133	4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
134	   ```bash
135	   git pull --rebase
136	   bd dolt push
137	   git push
138	   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
139	   ```
140	5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
141	6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
142	7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
143	
144	**CRITICAL RULES:**
145	- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
146	- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
147	- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
148	- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
149	
150	<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->
151	<!-- compound-agent:start -->
152	## Compound Agent Integration
153	
154	This project uses compound-agent for session memory via **CLI commands**.
155	
156	### CLI Commands (ALWAYS USE THESE)
157	
158	**You MUST use CLI commands for lesson management:**
159	
160	| Command | Purpose |
161	|---------|---------|
162	| `ca search "query"` | Search lessons - MUST call before architectural decisions; use anytime you need context |
163	| `ca knowledge "query"` | Semantic search over project docs - MUST call before architectural decisions; use keyword phrases, not questions |
164	| `ca learn "insight"` | Capture lessons - use AFTER corrections or discoveries |
165	| `ca list` | List all stored lessons |
166	| `ca show <id>` | Show details of a specific lesson |
167	| `ca wrong <id>` | Mark a lesson as incorrect |
168	
169	### Mandatory Recall
170	
171	You MUST call `ca search` and `ca knowledge` BEFORE:
172	- Architectural decisions or complex planning
173	- Patterns you've implemented before in this repo
174	- After user corrections ("actually...", "wrong", "use X instead")
175	
176	**NEVER skip search for complex decisions.** Past mistakes will repeat.
177	
178	Beyond mandatory triggers, use these commands freely — they are lightweight queries, not heavyweight operations. Uncertain about a pattern? `ca search`. Need a detail from the docs? `ca knowledge`. The cost of an unnecessary search is near-zero; the cost of a missed one can be hours.
179	
180	### Capture Protocol
181	
182	Run `ca learn` AFTER:
183	- User corrects you
184	- Test fail -> fix -> pass cycles
185	- You discover project-specific knowledge
186	
187	**Workflow**: Search BEFORE deciding, capture AFTER learning.
188	
189	### Quality Gate
190	
191	Before capturing, verify the lesson is:
192	- **Novel** - Not already stored
193	- **Specific** - Clear guidance
194	- **Actionable** (preferred) - Obvious what to do
195	
196	### Never Edit JSONL Directly
197	
198	**WARNING: NEVER edit .claude/lessons/index.jsonl directly.**
199	
200	The JSONL file requires proper ID generation, schema validation, and SQLite sync.
201	Use CLI (`ca learn`) — never manual edits.
202	
203	See [documentation](https://github.com/Nathandela/compound-agent) for more details.
204	<!-- compound-agent:end -->

3	description: Dark Research Lab - autonomous research paper factory forked from compound-agent, targeting social science
4	type: project
5	---
6	
7	Dark Research Lab (DRL) is a Claude Code harness for long-duration autonomous research paper production.
8	
9	**Why:** No existing tool targets social science research automation with methodological traceability. Existing tools (AI-Scientist-v2, EvoScientist, Kosmos) are all ML/STEM focused.
10	
11	**How to apply:** Every design decision should serve the goal of autonomous, traceable, publication-ready social science research. When in doubt, reference the compound-agent patterns and adapt for research context.
12	
13	Key properties:
14	- Ships as own pnpm plugin with forked Go binary (`drl` CLI)
15	- Namespace: `/drl:*` for all slash commands
16	- Dark factory autonomy: fully autonomous after spec, human reviews final output
17	- LaTeX output with auto-generated tables/figures
18	- PDF + RAG for literature (reusing ca-embed Rust daemon)
19	- Decision log for methodological traceability
20	- Multi-model advisory fleet (Claude + Gemini + Codex)
21	- Auto-generated reproducibility package with every paper
22	- Single researcher per project

1	---
2	name: Compound Agent Reference
3	description: Key patterns from Nathandela/compound-agent to reuse - file structure, skills, hooks, architect
4	type: reference
5	---
6	
7	Source repo: github.com/Nathandela/compound-agent
8	
9	Key patterns to copy and adapt:
10	- **Skill-as-instruction-file**: Commands are thin wrappers that Read SKILL.md. Survives context compaction.
11	- **5-phase cook-it**: Spec-Dev -> Plan -> Work -> Review -> Compound. Phase gates enforce sequencing.
12	- **Phase guard hook**: PreToolUse blocks Edit/Write if current phase's SKILL.md hasn't been read.
13	- **Architect skill**: 6-phase decomposition (Socratic, Spec, Decompose, Materialize, Launch, Polish) with 5 human gates and parallel subagent convoy.
14	- **Advisory fleet**: Spawns external model CLIs (claude, gemini, codex) as background processes for multi-perspective evaluation.
15	- **Infinity loop**: `ca loop` generates bash script processing beads epics in dependency order via screen sessions.
16	- **Memory**: JSONL + SQLite FTS5 + Rust ONNX embeddings. Auto-injected at SessionStart and PreCompact.
17	- **Hooks**: 7 hooks (SessionStart, PreCompact, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUseFailure, PostToolUse, PreToolUse, Stop).
18	- **Templates embedded**: Go's embed package bundles all templates. `ca setup` installs them.
19	
20	Go binary structure: `go/cmd/ca/main.go` + `go/internal/` (cli, capture, compound, embed, hook, knowledge, memory, retrieval, search, setup, storage, telemetry).
21

Compilation via 3-pass pdflatex + bibtex script.

14	
15	**Testing**: TDD with 2085 lines of tests matching src/ structure.
16	
17	**Pain points to fix in DRL**:
18	- Regression spec duplication (should be config-driven)
19	- Manual LaTeX compilation (should be integrated in pipeline)
20	- Scattered color/style definitions (should be centralized)
21	- No formal pipeline specification (DRL's architect + specs solve this)
22	- No decision logging (DRL's decision log solves this)

1	# DRL Package -- System Specification
2	
3	*Date: 2026-04-02*
4	*Status: Draft*
5	
6	## 1. System Overview
7	
8	The Dark Research Lab (DRL) is a PyPI-distributed CLI tool that turns any git repository into an autonomous research paper factory. It ships as a Go binary (`drl`) forked from compound-agent (`ca`), packaged inside platform-specific Python wheels (same pattern as ruff/uv). Install: `uv pip install drl`. The Go binary embeds all templates that `drl setup` installs into a project.
9	
10	**Primary actor**: A single social science researcher.
11	**System boundary**: The `drl` CLI + installed template files (skills, agents, commands, hooks, scaffolding).
12	
13	---
14	
15	## 2. EARS Requirements
16	
17	### 2.1 Ubiquitous (always true)
18	
19	- **U1**: The DRL CLI SHALL be distributed as a PyPI package containing a platform-specific Go binary named `drl`, installable via `uv pip install drl`.
20	- **U2**: All slash commands SHALL use the `/drl:*` namespace.
21	- **U3**: All skill files SHALL follow the skill-as-instruction-file pattern (thin command wrapper -> Read SKILL.md).
22	- **U4**: The system SHALL preserve all compound-agent subsystems: memory (JSONL), lessons, knowledge (SQLite FTS5), search (ca-embed), beads (bd), hooks, infinity loop.
23	- **U5**: Every methodological decision made during research work SHALL be logged to `docs/decisions/`.
24	- **U6**: The system SHALL auto-generate a reproducibility package (uv.lock + data manifest + run script + env spec) at paper compilation time.
25	
26	### 2.2 Event-Driven
27	
28	- **E1**: WHEN `drl setup` is executed in an empty repo, the system SHALL install ALL template files (.claude/, paper/, src/, docs/, literature/, tests/, AGENTS.md). WHEN executed in an existing DRL repo, the system SHALL update only infrastructure (hooks, config, scaffolding, commands) and preserve skill/agent customizations.
29	- **E1a**: WHEN `drl setup --core-skill` is executed, the system SHALL additionally update core workflow skills (architect, spec, plan, work, review, synthesis, flavor, onboard) and core agents (analyst, reproducibility-verifier), preserving style-sensitive skills/agents.
30	- **E1b**: WHEN `drl setup --all-skill` is executed, the system SHALL update ALL skills and agents, overwriting any flavor customizations.
31	- **E2**: WHEN a new PDF is added to `literature/pdfs/`, the system SHALL extract text, chunk, embed (via ca-embed), and index it in SQLite FTS5 + vector store.
32	- **E3**: WHEN a cook-it phase transition occurs, the system SHALL inject a reminder to log any pending methodological decisions.
33	- **E4**: WHEN `/drl:flavor` is invoked, the system SHALL interview the researcher about their field and directly edit skill files to customize methodology vocabulary, evidence standards, and writing conventions.
34	- **E5**: WHEN `/drl:compile` is invoked, the system SHALL run 3-pass pdflatex + bibtex, generate reproducibility package, and report any unresolved references or missing figures.
35	- **E6**: WHEN `/drl:onboard` is invoked, the system SHALL guide the researcher through project setup, suggest `/drl:flavor`, and explain the framework structure.
36	
37	### 2.3 State-Driven
38	
39	- **S1**: WHILE in the research-work phase, the system SHALL auto-log decisions to `docs/decisions/` and write agent progress notes to `docs/agent_notes/`.
40	- **S2**: WHILE the phase guard is active, the system SHALL block Edit/Write tool calls if the current phase's SKILL.md has not been read.
41	- **S3**: WHILE the infinity loop is running, the system SHALL process epic beads in dependency order, each through the full cook-it cycle.
42	
43	### 2.4 Unwanted Behavior
44	
45	- **W1**: IF the researcher attempts to run analysis without a research-spec approved, THEN the system SHALL block and prompt for spec completion.
46	- **W2**: IF a literature search returns zero results for a key claim, THEN the system SHALL flag the claim as unsupported in the methodology review.
47	- **W3**: IF LaTeX compilation fails, THEN the system SHALL report specific errors and not mark the synthesis phase as complete.
48	
49	### 2.5 Optional
50	
51	- **O1**: WHERE the researcher has provided field-specific flavor configuration, the system SHALL use adapted vocabulary, evidence standards, and citation style in all generated content.
52	- **O2**: WHERE external model CLIs are available (gemini, codex), the system SHALL include them in the advisory fleet during review phases.
53	
54	---
55	
56	## 3. Architecture Diagrams
57	
58	### 3.1 C4 Context
59	
60	```mermaid
61	C4Context
62	    title DRL System Context
63	
64	    Person(researcher, "Researcher", "Social science researcher")
65	
66	    System(drl, "DRL CLI", "Go binary + installed templates. Orchestrates autonomous research paper production.")
67	
68	    System_Ext(claude, "Claude Code", "AI assistant executing skills/agents")
69	    System_Ext(ca_embed, "ca-embed", "Rust ONNX daemon for text embeddings")
70	    System_Ext(beads, "Beads (bd)", "Issue tracking with dependency graphs")
71	    System_Ext(advisory, "Advisory Fleet", "External model CLIs: Gemini, Codex")
72	    System_Ext(latex, "LaTeX/BibTeX", "Paper compilation toolchain")
73	
74	    Rel(researcher, drl, "Configures project, provides research question, reviews output")
75	    Rel(drl, claude, "Installs skills/agents/hooks that Claude executes")
76	    Rel(drl, ca_embed, "Manages embedding daemon for literature RAG")
77	    Rel(drl, beads, "Creates/tracks research epic beads")
78	    Rel(drl, advisory, "Spawns external model reviewers")
79	    Rel(drl, latex, "Compiles paper via pdflatex + bibtex")
80	```
81	
82	### 3.2 Sequence: Full Research Cycle
83	
84	```mermaid
85	sequenceDiagram
86	    participant R as Researcher
87	    participant DRL as drl CLI
88	    participant C as Claude Code
89	    participant BD as Beads
90	    participant RAG as Literature RAG
91	    participant TEX as LaTeX
92	
93	    R->>DRL: drl setup
94	    DRL->>DRL: Install templates (.claude/, paper/, src/, docs/, literature/)
95	
96	    R->>DRL: /drl:onboard
97	    DRL->>C: Read onboard SKILL.md
98	    C->>R: Guide through setup, suggest /drl:flavor
99	
100	    R->>DRL: /drl:flavor
101	    DRL->>C: Read flavor SKILL.md
102	    C->>R: Interview about field
103	    C->>C: Edit skill files with field conventions
104	
105	    R->>DRL: Drop PDFs in literature/pdfs/
106	    DRL->>RAG: drl index (extract, chunk, embed, store)
107	
108	    R->>DRL: /drl:architect
109	    DRL->>C: Read research-architect SKILL.md
110	    C->>BD: Create epic beads with dependencies
111	    C->>R: Approve epic structure
112	
113	    R->>DRL: drl loop (infinity loop)
114	    loop Each Epic (dependency order)
115	        DRL->>C: /drl:spec (research question, hypotheses)
116	        C->>RAG: Search literature for context
117	        DRL->>C: /drl:plan (methodology, variables, models)
118	        DRL->>C: /drl:work (analysis, tables, figures, writing)
119	        C->>C: Auto-log decisions to docs/decisions/
120	        DRL->>C: /drl:review (methodology review + advisory fleet)
121	        DRL->>C: /drl:compound (synthesis + lessons)
122	        DRL->>BD: Close epic bead
123	    end
124	
125	    DRL->>C: /drl:compile
126	    C->>TEX: 3-pass pdflatex + bibtex
127	    C->>DRL: Generate reproducibility package
128	    DRL->>R: Paper PDF + reproducibility archive
129	```
130	
131	### 3.3 State: Cook-It Cycle (Research-Adapted)
132	
133	```mermaid
134	stateDiagram-v2
135	    [*] --> ResearchSpec: /drl:spec
136	    ResearchSpec --> ResearchPlan: Spec approved
137	    ResearchPlan --> ResearchWork: Plan approved
138	    ResearchWork --> MethodologyReview: Analysis complete
139	    MethodologyReview --> Synthesis: Review passes
140	    MethodologyReview --> ResearchWork: Review fails (rework)
141	    Synthesis --> [*]: Paper section complete
142	
143	    state ResearchSpec {
144	        [*] --> DefineRQ: Define research question
145	        DefineRQ --> StateHypotheses: RQ clear
146	        StateHypotheses --> LitGap: Hypotheses stated
147	        LitGap --> SpecGate: Gap identified
148	        SpecGate --> [*]: Human approves
149	    }
150	
151	    state ResearchWork {
152	        [*] --> RunAnalysis
153	        RunAnalysis --> GenerateOutputs: Analysis done
154	        GenerateOutputs --> WriteSections: Tables/figures ready
155	        WriteSections --> LogDecisions: Sections drafted
156	        LogDecisions --> [*]: All planned work complete
157	    }
158	
159	    state MethodologyReview {
160	        [*] --> StatisticalCheck
161	        StatisticalCheck --> LogicalCheck
162	        LogicalCheck --> CitationCheck
163	        CitationCheck --> ReproCheck
164	        ReproCheck --> WritingCheck
165	        WritingCheck --> [*]: All checks pass/fail
166	    }
167	```
168	
169	---
170	
171	## 4. Scenario Table
172	
173	| ID | Scenario | EARS Req | Expected Outcome |
174	|---|---|---|---|
175	| SC1 | Researcher runs `drl setup` in empty repo | E1 | All template dirs/files created |
176	| SC2 | Researcher drops 5 PDFs and runs `drl index` | E2 | PDFs extracted, chunked, embedded, searchable via `drl knowledge` |
177	| SC3 | Agent searches literature for "wage elasticity" | E2, S1 | Returns relevant passages from indexed PDFs |
178	| SC4 | Agent makes a variable selection during work phase | U5, S1, E3 | Decision logged to docs/decisions/, reminder shown at next phase |
179	| SC5 | Full cook-it cycle on one epic | S3, U3 | 5 phases execute in order, phase guard enforced |
180	| SC6 | `/drl:flavor` for labor economics | E4, O1 | Skills updated with labor econ vocabulary and conventions |
181	| SC7 | `/drl:compile` with missing figure | W3 | Compilation fails, specific error reported |
182	| SC8 | `/drl:compile` succeeds | E5, U6 | PDF generated, reproducibility package created |
183	| SC9 | Infinity loop processes 6 epics | S3, U4 | Epics processed in dependency order, each through cook-it |
184	| SC10 | Phase guard blocks edit before skill read | S2 | Edit/Write rejected with guidance to read skill first |

1	# Dark Research Lab - Exploration Synthesis
2	
3	*Date: 2026-04-02*
4	
5	## 1. What We're Building
6	
7	A **Claude Code harness for long-duration autonomous research paper production**, targeting social science research. Distributed as its own pnpm plugin (like compound-agent's `ca`), with the command namespace `/drl:*`.
8	
9	The system takes a research question as input and autonomously produces a publication-ready LaTeX paper, with full methodological traceability via decision logs.
10	
11	### Core Principle
12	
13	> Copy the compound-agent infrastructure, then adapt the key elements from software development to academic research.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## 2. Architecture Decisions
18	
19	| Decision | Choice | Source |
20	|---|---|---|
21	| Domain | Social sciences (economics, mixed methods, statistical analysis) | User choice |
22	| Duration | Variable - architect + infinity-loop + polish handles any duration | User choice |
23	| Autonomy | Dark factory - fully autonomous after spec, human reviews final output | User choice |
24	| Output | LaTeX paper with auto-generated tables/figures | User choice |
25	| Architect | Research question decomposition into flexible epics | User choice |
26	| Literature | PDF folder + RAG via compound-agent's embed system (Rust daemon + SQLite FTS5) | User choice |
27	| Flavors | Interactive guided session where Claude edits skills/agents directly | User choice |
28	| Traceability | Auto decision log + hook reminders at phase transitions | User choice |
29	| Binary | Own pnpm plugin, copying ca infrastructure and adapting for research | User choice |
30	| Phases | Flexible (architect decides epic structure) + cook-it cycle inside each epic | User choice |
31	| Notes | Structured channels: `researcher_notes/` (human) + `agent_notes/` (agent) | User choice |
32	| Onboarding | Guided setup wizard (`/drl:onboard`) | User choice |
33	| LaTeX | Pre-scaffolded template shipped with framework | User choice |
34	| Data pipeline | Framework provides src/ scaffolding, agent fills in project-specific logic | User choice |
35	| Namespace | `/drl:*` | User choice |
36	
37	---
38	
39	## 3. What We Keep from Compound-Agent
40	
41	### Unchanged Infrastructure
42	- **ca binary core**: Memory, lessons, knowledge, search (JSONL + SQLite FTS5 + Rust embed daemon)
43	- **Beads (bd)**: Issue tracking with dependency graphs for epic management
44	- **5-phase cook-it cycle**: Spec-Dev -> Plan -> Work -> Review -> Compound
45	- **Phase gates + guard hooks**: PreToolUse blocks edits if skill not read
46	- **Infinity loop**: `ca loop` processes beads epics autonomously in sequence
47	- **Hook system**: SessionStart prime, PreCompact reload, UserPromptSubmit patterns, PostToolUseFailure tracking, phase guard
48	- **Memory/lessons JSONL**: Git-tracked, conflict-free, auto-injected at session start
49	
50	### Reused Patterns
51	- **Skill-as-instruction-file**: Commands are thin wrappers -> Read SKILL.md (survives compaction)
52	- **Parallel subagent decomposition**: Architect spawns 6+ agents via Task tool, synthesizes findings
53	- **Advisory fleet**: External model CLIs evaluate from different lenses
54	- **Improvement loop**: Iterative `improve/*.md` programs for polish cycles
55	
56	---
57	
58	## 4. What Changes
59	
60	### Skills (rewrite for research context)
61	
62	| Compound-Agent Skill | Dark Research Lab Equivalent | Setup Tier |
63	|---|---|---|
64	| `spec-dev` (feature specification) | `research-spec` (research question, hypotheses, methodology outline) | Core |
65	| `plan` (implementation plan) | `research-plan` (analysis plan, data requirements, statistical approach) | Core |
66	| `work` (code implementation) | `research-work` (literature analysis, data processing, statistical analysis, writing) | Core |
67	| `review` (code review) | `methodology-review` (statistical validity, logical consistency, citation accuracy) | Core |
68	| `compound` (lesson extraction) | `synthesis` (cross-section coherence, contribution clarity, findings consolidation) | Core |
69	| `architect` (system decomposition) | `research-architect` (research question decomposition into epics) | Core |
70	| `get-a-phd` (deep research) | `lit-review` (systematic literature review with PDF RAG) | Style |
71	| -- | `flavor` (interactive field customization) | Core |
72	| -- | `onboard` (guided setup wizard) | Core |
73	
74	### Agents (replace code-focused with research-focused)
75	
76	| Compound-Agent Agent | Dark Research Lab Equivalent | Setup Tier |
77	|---|---|---|
78	| `implementer` | `analyst` (runs statistical analysis, generates tables/figures) | Core |
79	| `test-writer` | `robustness-checker` (designs robustness checks, alternative specifications) | Style |
80	| `security-reviewer` | `methodology-reviewer` (checks statistical validity, assumption violations) | Style |
81	| `architecture-reviewer` | `coherence-reviewer` (checks paper structure, argument flow, cross-references) | Style |
82	| `runtime-verifier` | `reproducibility-verifier` (checks that results can be reproduced from data + code) | Core |
83	| `research-specialist` | `literature-analyst` (searches embedded PDFs, synthesizes findings) | Style |
84	| `lint-classifier` | `citation-checker` (validates references, checks for missing citations) | Style |
85	| `doc-gardener` | `writing-quality-reviewer` (prose quality, academic voice, clarity) | Style |
86	
87	### Setup Tiers
88	
89	Templates are classified into three tiers to protect flavor customizations during updates:
90	
91	| Tier | Flag | Behavior |
92	|---|---|---|
93	| **Infrastructure** | `drl setup` | Always updates: hooks, settings.json, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, paper/, src/, docs/, literature/, tests/, all /drl:* commands. First install in empty repo ships everything. |
94	| **Core** | `drl setup --core-skill` | Additionally updates: structural workflow skills (architect, spec, plan, work, review, synthesis, flavor, onboard) + core agents (analyst, reproducibility-verifier). |
95	| **Full** | `drl setup --all-skill` | Updates ALL skills and agents, overwriting flavor customizations. |
96	
97	**Design rationale**: Researchers use `/drl:flavor` to customize style-sensitive skills/agents for their field. Base `drl setup` must never overwrite those customizations. Core workflow skills define the process machinery and are field-agnostic. Style skills/agents contain field-dependent vocabulary, evidence standards, and writing conventions.
98	
99	### New Commands
100

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the setup implementation. Let me compile a detailed report.

## Setup Command Implementation Report

### 1. Command Flow (commands_setup.go)

**Key Entry Points:**

Two top-level commands delegate to `setup.InitRepo()`:
- `init` - Full initialization with hooks + templates
- `setup` - Shorthand that calls the same logic with default options

**Flag Structure:**
```go
type InitOptions struct {
    SkipHooks     bool        // Skip Claude Code hook installation
    SkipTemplates bool        // Skip agent/command/skill/doc templates
    BinaryPath    string      // Path to Go binary for hook commands
}
```

**Flow (commands_setup.go):**
1. `initCmd()` / `setupCmd()` → parses flags
2. Calls `setup.InitRepo(repoRoot, InitOptions)` → returns `*InitResult`
3. `*InitResult` contains success flags, counts of created/updated items, hook status

### 2. Core Setup Logic (init.go)

**Execution Order in `InitRepo()`:**
```go
1. initDirectories()         // Create .claude/ structure, lessons/index.jsonl
2. [optional] initHooks()    // Install/upgrade Claude Code hooks
3. [optional] installTemplates()
   ├─ UpdateAgentsMd()       // Update AGENTS.md with section
   ├─ EnsureClaudeMdReference()  // Add reference to .claude/CLAUDE.md
   ├─ CreatePluginManifest()     // Create .claude/plugin.json with version
   └─ installTemplateGroups()
       ├─ InstallAgentTemplates()
       ├─ InstallWorkflowCommands()
       ├─ InstallPhaseSkills()     // Substitutes quality gates
       ├─ InstallAgentRoleSkills()
       ├─ InstallDocTemplates()    // Substitutes {{VERSION}}, {{DATE}}, quality gates
       ├─ InstallResearchDocs()    // Walk embedded tree
       ├─ PruneStaleTemplates()    // Remove retired files
       └─ CompileSkillsIndex()     // Generate skills_index.json
```

**Key Patterns:**
- **Idempotent design**: Uses markers like `CompoundAgentSectionHeader` to detect existing sections
- **Date normalization**: Docs ignore date-only differences in updates (`normalizeDocDate()`)
- **Quality gate substitution**: Replaces `{{QUALITY_GATE_*}}` placeholders with detected stack commands
- **Reconciliation**: Each file is checked against existing content before updating (`reconcileFile()`)

### 3. Helper Functions (primitives.go)

**Core Primitives:**
```go
reconcileFile(path, content)  // Create or update if content differs
                              // Returns (created, updated, error)

installMapToDir(dir, files)   // Write map of filename→content to directory
                              // Returns (created, updated, error)

writeSkillFile(path, content) // Ensures parent dirs exist, calls reconcileFile()

substituteQualityGates(content, stack)  // Replace placeholders with stack commands
```

**Indexing:**
```go
CompileSkillsIndex(repoRoot)  // Pre-compiles skills_index.json from SKILL.md frontmatter
                              // Extracts: name, description, phase from YAML frontmatter
                              // Writes to .claude/skills/compound/skills_index.json
```

**Pruning Logic:**
```go
PruneStaleTemplates()         // Removes files/dirs that no longer exist in embedded templates
├─ pruneFlatDirs()            // Agents, commands, docs
├─ pruneResearchInternals()   // Research docs tree
├─ pruneStaleSubdirs()        // Phase/role directories
└─ pruneEntry()               // Recursive file/dir pruning with skipTopLevel support
```

### 4. Stack Detection (detect.go)

**`DetectStack(repoRoot) StackInfo`** - Checks for marker files in priority order:
```
go.mod    → go test ./..., golangci-lint run ./..., go build ./...
Cargo.toml → cargo test, cargo clippy, cargo build
pyproject.toml/setup.py → pytest, ruff check ., python -m compileall .
package.json → detectNodePackageManager() [pnpm/yarn/bun/npm]
Makefile  → make test, make lint, make build
[default] → Fallback guidance messages
```

**Returns:**
```go
type StackInfo struct {
    TestCmd  string   // e.g., "go test ./..."
    LintCmd  string   // e.g., "golangci-lint run ./..."
    BuildCmd string   // e.g., "go build ./..."
}
```

Used to substitute quality gate placeholders in skills and docs.

### 5. Template Embedding (embed.go)

**Embed Strategy:**
```go
//go:embed agents/*.md
//go:embed commands/*.md
//go:embed skills              // Walks tree: skills/<phase>/SKILL.md
//go:embed agent-role-skills   // Walks tree: agent-role-skills/<role>/SKILL.md
//go:embed docs/*.md
//go:embed docs/research       // Full tree embedded
//go:embed agents-md.md, claude-md-reference.md, plugin.json
```

**Key Functions:**
```go
AgentTemplates()              // Map of filename → content (flat agents/)
CommandTemplates()            // Map of filename → content (flat commands/)
PhaseSkills()                 // Map of phase-name → SKILL.md content
PhaseSkillReferences()        // Map of "phase/rel-path" → content
AgentRoleSkills()             // Map of role-name → SKILL.md content
AgentRoleSkillReferences()    // Map of "role/rel-path" → content
DocTemplates()                // Map of filename → content (flat docs/)
ResearchDocs()                // Map of "rel/path" → content (full tree)
```

**Helper:**
```go
readEmbedDir(fs, dir)  // Shallow read of flat directory
```

### 6. Claude Code Settings & Hooks (settings.go)

**Hook Installation Architecture:**

```go
type managedHookSpec struct {
    hookType     string
    matcher      string        // Tool matcher (e.g., "Bash|Edit|Write", "Read")
    markers      []string      // Markers to detect existing hooks
    buildCommand func(binaryPath) string
}
```

**Managed Hook Types (7 total):**
1. `SessionStart` / `PreCompact` - `BIN prime` (priming context)
2. `UserPromptSubmit` - user-prompt handler
3. `PostToolUseFailure` - post-tool-failure handler (matcher: Bash|Edit|Write)
4. `PostToolUse` - post-tool-success (matcher: Bash|Edit|Write)
5. `PostToolUse` - post-read (matcher: Read)
6. `PreToolUse` - phase-guard (matcher: Edit|Write)
7. `Stop` - phase-audit (stop handler)

**Command Building:**
```go
makeHookCommand(binaryPath, hookName)
  → binary path: "{escaped-path} hooks run {hookName} 2>/dev/null || true"
  → npx fallback: "npx ca hooks run {hookName} 2>/dev/null || true"

makePrimeCommand(binaryPath)
  → binary path: "{escaped-path} prime 2>/dev/null || true"
  → npx fallback: "npx ca prime 2>/dev/null || true"
```

**Hook Management Functions:**
```go
ReadClaudeSettings(path)         // Returns map[string]any or empty if missing
WriteClaudeSettings(path, set)   // Atomic write (write-temp → rename)
HasAllHooks(settings)            // Check all 7 hooks installed with markers
HooksNeedUpgrade(settings, bin)  // Detect npx commands when binary available
HooksNeedDedupe(settings)        // Detect duplicate hook entries
AddAllHooks(settings, binaryPath) // Install/reconcile all hooks
RemoveAllHooks(settings)         // Remove all compound-agent hooks

// Internal:
getHooksMap(settings)            // Get or create hooks map
getHookArray(hooks, type)        // Get or create type array
hasHookMarker(arr, markers)      // Check for marker in hook array
filterHookEntries(arr, markers)  // Remove entries matching markers
upgradeNpxHooks(hooks, binary)   // Replace npx with binary path
isCompoundHookEntry(entry)       // Check if entry is managed
```

---

## Where to Add Tier Support

### Modification Strategy for Tier Annotations

**1. Extended InitOptions (init.go)**
```go
type InitOptions struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    SkipHooks     bool
    SkipTemplates bool
    BinaryPath    string
    
    // NEW: Tier support
    Tier          string   // "infrastructure" | "core" | "style"
    TierFilters   map[string]bool
}
```

**2. Template Tier Annotations (embed.go)**

Each template source needs tier metadata:
```go
type TieredTemplate struct {
    Name    string
    Content string
    Tier    string   // "infrastructure" | "core" | "style"
}

// NEW: Add tier-aware accessors
func AgentTemplates(tier string) map[string]string  // Filter by tier
func PhaseSkills(tier string) map[string]string     // Filter by tier
```

**3. Tier Filtering in Installation (primitives.go)**

Wrap existing install functions:
```go
// NEW: 
func InstallAgentTemplatesFiltered(repoRoot, tier string) (int, int, error)

// Filter logic before installMapToDir():
func filterByTier(files map[string]string, tierFilter map[string]bool) map[string]string
```

**4. Hook Tier Filtering (settings.go)**

Hooks likely stay universal (all tiers use them), but could parameterize:
```go
// NEW: Optional tier-aware hook specs
func getManagedHookSpecs(tier string) []managedHookSpec
```

**5. Directory Structure for Tiers (init.go)**

Option A - Single tier:
```
.claude/
├─ agents/compound/<tier>/
├─ skills/compound/<tier>/
```

Option B - All tiers (union):
```
.claude/
├─ agents/compound/   # All tiers installed
├─ agents/tiers.json  # Metadata tracking which tier each agent belongs to
```

**6. Tier Manifest (new file)**

Create `.claude/tiers.json`:
```json
{
  "activeConfiguration": "core",
  "availableTiers": ["infrastructure", "core", "style"],
  "installed": {
    "infrastructure": {
      "agents": 3,
      "skills": 5,
      "commands": 2
    },
    "core": {
      "agents": 4,
      "skills": 8,
      "commands": 3
    }
  }
}
```

**7. Prune Logic Updates (primitives.go)**

Extend `PruneStaleTemplates()` to respect tier membership:
```go
// NEW:
func PruneStaleTemplates(repoRoot, activeTier string) (int, error)
  // Only remove files not in activeTier or universal tiers
```

**8. CLI Flag Extension (commands_setup.go)**

```go
func initCmd() {
    var (
        skipHooks  bool
        skipAgents bool
        tier       string  // NEW
        allTiers   bool    // NEW: install all tiers
    )
    
    cmd.Flags().StringVar(&tier, "tier", "core", "Tier: infrastructure|core|style")
    cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&allTiers, "all-tiers", false, "Install all tiers")
}
```

**9. Doctor Command Extension (commands_setup.go)**

New diagnostic:
```go
checkTiers(repoRoot)  // Returns active tier, available tiers, completeness
```

---

## Function Signature Summary

**Public Entry Points (would need tier support):**
```go
InitRepo(repoRoot string, opts InitOptions) (*InitResult, error)
InstallAgentTemplates(repoRoot string) (int, int, error)
InstallPhaseSkills(repoRoot string, stack StackInfo) (int, int, error)
InstallDocTemplates(repoRoot, version string, stack StackInfo) (int, int, error)
PruneStaleTemplates(repoRoot string) (int, error)
CompileSkillsIndex(repoRoot string) error
```

**Key Data Structures:**
```go
InitResult { Success, HooksInstalled, DirsCreated, FilesCreated, 
             AgentsInstalled/Updated, CommandsInstalled/Updated, ... }
StackInfo { TestCmd, LintCmd, BuildCmd }
```

This implementation is highly modular—tier support can be added as a filtering/selection layer on top of the existing template installation pipeline without major refactoring.

```json
  }
}
```

**7. Prune Logic Updates (primitives.go)**

Extend `PruneStaleTemplates()` to respect tier membership:
```go
// NEW:
func PruneStaleTemplates(repoRoot, activeTier string) (int, error)
  // Only remove files not in activeTier or universal tiers
```

**8. CLI Flag Extension (commands_setup.go)**

```go
func initCmd() {

        skipAgents bool
        tier       string  // NEW
        allTiers   bool    // NEW: install all tiers
    )

    cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&allTiers, "all-tiers", false, "Install all tiers")
}
```

**9. Doctor Command Extension (commands_setup.go)**

New diagnostic:
```go
checkTiers(repoRoot)  // Returns active tier, available tiers, completeness
```

---

## Function Signature Summary

**Public Entry Points (would need tier support):**
```go
InitRepo(repoRoot string, opts InitOptions) (*InitResult, error)
InstallAgentTemplates(repoRoot string) (int, int, error)
InstallPhaseSkills(repoRoot string, stack StackInfo) (int, int, error)
InstallDocTemplates(repoRoot, version string, stack StackInfo) (int, int, error)
PruneStaleTemplates(repoRoot string) (int, error)
CompileSkillsIndex(repoRoot string) error
```

**Key Data Structures:**
```go
InitResult { Success, HooksInstalled, DirsCreated, FilesCreated, 
             AgentsInstalled/Updated, CommandsInstalled/Updated, ... }
StackInfo { TestCmd, LintCmd, BuildCmd }
```

This implementation is highly modular—tier support can be added as a filtering/selection layer on top of the existing template installation pipeline without major refactoring.

## Dark Research Lab - Project Exploration Report

### Project Overview

Dark Research Lab (DRL) is an autonomous research paper factory forked from **compound-agent**, targeting social science research automation. It ships as a Go binary with PyPI distribution (like ruff/uv), providing researchers with a `/drl:*` CLI namespace and autonomous research workflow orchestration.

**Status**: Post-exploration phase (completed 2026-04-02). Architecture decisions locked. Architect phase complete. Ready for implementation.

---

### Key Architecture Decisions (Locked)

All 18 decisions documented in `/Users/Nathan/.claude/projects/-Users-Nathan-Documents-Code-dark-research-lab/memory/architecture_decisions.md`:

| **Domain** | Social sciences (economics, mixed methods, statistical analysis) |
| **Duration** | Variable - architect + infinity-loop + polish |
| **Autonomy** | Dark factory (fully autonomous after spec, human reviews final) |
| **Output** | LaTeX paper with auto-generated tables/figures |
| **Distribution** | PyPI (Go binary in Python wheels, pattern: ruff/uv) |
| **Architecture** | Research question decomposition into flexible epics |
| **Literature** | PDF folder + RAG via ca-embed (Rust ONNX daemon + SQLite FTS5) |
| **Customization** | Interactive `/drl:flavor` for field-specific vocabulary/evidence standards |
| **Traceability** | Auto decision log + hook reminders at phase transitions |
| **Binary** | Fork ca Go code into independent 'drl' binary |
| **Phases** | Flexible (architect decides) + cook-it inside each epic |
| **Notes** | Structured: `researcher_notes/` (human) + `agent_notes/` (agent) |
| **Onboarding** | Guided setup wizard (`/drl:onboard`) |
| **LaTeX** | Pre-scaffolded template |
| **Data pipeline** | Framework provides `src/` scaffolding |
| **Namespace** | `/drl:*` |
| **Collaboration** | Single researcher only |
| **External models** | Keep full multi-model advisory fleet |
| **Reproducibility** | Always auto-generate package |

### Constraints & Decisions

#### Tier System for Setup Updates
- **Infrastructure** (no flag): Always updates hooks, settings, commands, scaffolding, paper/, src/, docs/, literature/
- **Core** (`--core-skill`): Additionally updates: architect, spec, plan, work, review, synthesis, flavor, onboard skills + analyst/reproducibility-verifier agents
- **Full** (`--all-skill`): Updates everything including style-sensitive skills/agents
- **Rationale**: Researchers customize via `/drl:flavor`. Base setup must never overwrite field-specific customizations.
